speaker: interviewer
text: A [NON-ENGLISH], or--

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. Like a big--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you were sent to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. How do you call it? In the front of a building, you have a big square.

speaker: interviewer
text: Square, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to walk to a big square. But what I forgot to mention, a time-- a certain time before we left, there was a time when the Germans suddenly decided everything should be beautiful in the ghetto. We had a theater. We had, suddenly, musicians to-- to play in front of us. There was-- we had better food. We had to clean the ghetto, and they told us that Red Cross will come to visit. So before they came, everything had to shine. Everything had to be good. They ask us how we feel. And they let us be more together with the parents. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everything was fun and shiny.

speaker: interviewer
text: For how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- It was probably for two weeks. I can't remember exactly the time. But after the visit, everything went back like it was before. It was probably specially made up to show the world that the people live nice, and everything. They have everything they need. They have music. We could play soccer. And we had a lot of good soccer players in the ghetto, too, from Vienna. I remember that, during this time, too, they played soccer in the front of the SS. The SS were watching how the Jewish soccer player played. And they make two teams. And they played soccer in the ghetto, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the kind of food you received every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I really--

speaker: interviewer
text: The rationing, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what-- what food-- in-- in the ghetto, I don't remember what we got.

speaker: interviewer
text: It wasn't enough?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing I know we were hungry. This I know, because we looked always to find potatoes, or whatever we could find, so we have something to eat. But later on, to compare this ghetto Theresienstadt to a concentration camp, it's like a difference between day or night. There's no question that a ghetto cannot be compared to a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the sanitation of the ghetto? There were showers, or bathrooms?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had-- we had-- in the [NON-ENGLISH], we had toilets, first of all. We had a shower, from time to time. We had a bathroom that we could wash ourself. I don't remember exactly if we had a shower in it. But we could wash ourself. We kept clean. If you had lice in the ghetto, I don't know. I don't remember that. But I think in the ghetto we had-- it was much better than in the concentration camp. Sure, you were not free. You were in the walls in the ghetto. You couldn't go out. You couldn't do nothing. You could visit another [NON-ENGLISH], to visit your father and mother. But we were all together in this room. We could go sometimes out to play. But-- but it was not the worst in the world that we found out later, it was no comparison. To make the story that we had to go, the [INAUDIBLE] told the Hacker family it is too big. It was-- when we heard that we had to go [INAUDIBLE] it was terrible, because I couldn't walk. I had crutches under my feet. My whole body was burned. I had wounds. And my mother-- my mother was sick. And she was worried about me, how I could go. And she kept holding on one side. Even she was sick, and after the operation, she holded one hand and my father hold the other hand. And we walked to the square. And from the square, we had-- then they had-- already had the railroad was coming in already to the ghetto. Again, we were loaded on wagons, closed up. Then it was already more people. We had already less suitcases, that we came from Vienna. We could not-- we were not allowed to take more than, I think, one or two suitcases. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were packed in there in these trains, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] again, without food, without water, without toilets. And I don't remember how long it took, if it took two days, three, or four. It looked like four years that we were in the trains. It was so terrible. It was smelling. It was hunger. It was crying. It was people that were sick, elderly, kids, all stuffed together. And I'm-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember how long it took. But suddenly we arrived in a place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when they opened the doors, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and we saw fences, and we heard screaming, and we saw dogs with the SS, and people in grey and blue striped clothing, with round heads. Everything was the same-- stripes, grey and blue, with sticks, with guns, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the wires, and the dogs. And they'd start screaming. Raus, Juden, raus. Schnell! [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I couldn't walk, but my father and my mother took me and pushed me down. And they came with sticks. How fast we did it, it was not fast enough. They start hitting. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And whatever we had in the hand, we had to throw everything on one big mountain. And the screaming was so bad, we start crying. My father run after the handbag that they took out of his arm and threw it on the mountain of suitcases and handbags. And an SS, with his handgun, start screaming him to let it go. If not, he shoots him. I was 13 years old, a little bit older. No, I was 15 years old. With my sticks, and my mother on the side, I was walking, jumping to my death. And I took the handbag, and I threw it on the mountain. I says, dad-- papa, I called him-- papa. You want to die because of this suitcase, I told him. Don't die because it. I took it. I threw it on the mountain, and I took my father back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was terrible. All the people had to go out of the train screaming, yelling. [GERMAN]. Go in a row, they screamed, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and hit, and yelled. And the dogs were barking at us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And what we saw a-- a camp-- fences, and every certain places was a SS little-- a little--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tower?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little-- one story for the guards, with machine guns on top. It was a metal, square construction. And on top of it was like a little house with a machine gun, with a German guard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Guard towers.

speaker: interviewee
text: All around. And we saw-- from far away, we saw buildings [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with-- coming out of the building there was a high stack [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like a factory, a big factory with chimney, a big chimney, high-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] going to the sky, with chimneys and coming smoke out. What we didn't know what it is, if it was a factory to work there. But not one. There were many-- maybe four, maybe five. Smoke came out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And even-- not only the SS, the Jewish guard-- there was guards that were clothed in concentration camp clothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Striped uniforms.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was screaming at us. Make-- we had to go in rows and start walking. And we walked about half an hour. They put us in a building. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They put it-- they called this [GERMAN] building. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a building that-- where they had to clean us from lice. They put us in special water. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was like Lysol water. It was special to-- to-- if you had lice or flies or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Disinfecting.

speaker: interviewee
text: Disinfection building what it called, where they shaved us. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they took-- anyway, from the train on, we had nothing. i-- they took our clothes and gave us concentration camp clothes we had to wear. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they left-- they left us together, the whole families. And we had to start to walk to another camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: We was-- we were shocked. I mean, it-- we couldn't believe what happened with us. If they're going to kill us-- what happen? Where we are? It looked like the end of the world. Everything wires, and then we found out electric wires, that if you touch it, you die. You were not separated?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- no. We were not separated at that time. And they walked us in [GERMAN]. We came into Birkenau. Birkenau was the camp that were the factories of death. It was Birkenau to Auschwitz. Auschwitz was the management camp. They had there a crematorium too, but for people that died-- Polacks, Ukraines, if they-- there were a lot of inmates from different countries because they were criminals, homosexuals, communists. But they were kapos. They were in the [INAUDIBLE] mostly in Auschwitz, and they were working for the Germans. But Birkenau by itself was a camp that was done only to gas the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was an extermination camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Extermination camp that they brought us in [GERMAN]. They called us, [GERMAN]. I was separated from my mother. And I knew, right away, that if I will go with the sticks, I will not live long. This-- I had a feeling from the screaming, from the hitting. So after I went the-- the-- through the first barrack, I lost--

speaker: interviewer
text: The crutches.

speaker: interviewee
text: I lost the crutches, and I-- I-- however I could. My father helped me. And I was in a block together with my father and my brother. My-- the womans were in a separate block. This I remember, in [GERMAN] B, in Birkenau. It was a [GERMAN] that all the families that came on first, second, and third transport, they concentrated in [GERMAN]. And they left us together. My father start to be sick, too. I start working in the barrack. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The barrack was a block-- was a building. In the middle was brick-- from one side to the other, a [INAUDIBLE]. It's like a-- like a fireplace, a heating place. I-- I don't-- never remember it was heated. But it was a heating place. And on both sides, there was a walkway that you could walk, I don't know, two, three feet wide, on both sides. And on both sides, you have beds, three story, one next to the other. There was no room. The only way you could get in in the front, from the bed. And there were laying probably four, five, or six people, one next to the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there mattresses on it?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no mattresses. On-- on-- there was nothing to lay on it. Maybe the-- I-- I-- I don't remember. There was no mattresses. There was nothing. And I start working there, at night. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the barrack, we had drums, like barrels-- barrel drums. And the people, if they had to urinate or whatever, they had to go in and do it on these drums. I worked at night to take out these drums, and bring it. There was another block that was used for toilets and this. And I used to bring it there, and turn it in there. For this job, I was-- I got-- I was rewarded. That the next day, after they gave to the people in the blocks the food, I could lick the barrel from the soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lick the barrel.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a big reward, because I could lick the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The barrel.

speaker: interviewee
text: The barrels. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Walter was with me, and his father was. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My-- my father was start to be already sick. He got water in the feet. One of the things that mostly I remember, and this was my mother-- she had a heart. Everybody loved her in the family. But she-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from her little bread that was-- she was separated at night, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she left her barrack, that if they would catch her they would hit her to death, probably, and she came to our barrack, and brought my father a little piece of bread. We had-- the only thing we got was a little piece. And from this little piece, she came at night and brought to my father a little piece of bread. I never forget how-- what a heart my mother would-- had, to do such a thing, from-- She didn't have nothing to eat, and from this little piece, she brought it to my father. [GERMAN]-- it was terrible. Food-wise, we had to go in the morning, early to-- we had to stay in Appell. They counted us.

speaker: interviewer
text: For how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: You had to stay sometime for hours. They counted everybody. It was October, November. It was very cold. It was in Poland. The camp was in Poland, not far from Cracow. It was cold. We didn't have nothing on, only the clothing they gave us. We have no underwear. They didn't give us shoes. We had wooden shoes-- [GERMAN], they called it. It was Appellplatz. We had to stay outside the barrack, every barrack. And then we had to-- we had to had to be counted. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- because I worked at night, during the day I did nothing. When they gave the food, I could lick the-- the-- [INAUDIBLE] barrels. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were there together-- October, we came there. No, January. I'm sorry. October, we came from [INAUDIBLE]. In January, 1945, we came to Birkenau-- January, 1945. We were-- they left us together, and assuming-- assuming they want, probably, to bring the Red Cross to-- to-- to Auschwitz or Birkenau, to show that the families are together. I mean, that they didn't-- the women were separate than the men, but still, they were in one camp. And it took about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] til July, beginning July--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you said January '45, you meant January, '44--

speaker: interviewee
text: '44, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --To July '44.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean January, '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: January, '44, yes. In July-- first of all, when I worked at night, and I took out [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the barrels and this, I saw the fire from the crematoriums, burning at night til heaven. And I still didn't believe. We didn't know, even [GERMAN] was probably 800 meters, above 2,000 feet, from one of the crematoriums. And during the night, I could even hear, from-- from this far, screaming from people. And I could smell. I still didn't know that they kill-- that they gassed people in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know. We ask. We-- we didn't have no communication with other barracks. They kept us separate, because everywhere it was electric wires. Even in B Lager already, some people couldn't decide, when sometimes in the morning, when we woke up, we saw people on the fence, that they went and put their hand up. And the moment you put your hand up, you were dead. People couldn't live with it anymore. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were so shocked. They were so afraid of everything that a lot of people committed suicide. And we had a wagon in-- in the B Lager that when people died, we had to collect them. People collect them, and brought them to the [GERMAN]. But they didn't let us out. In B Lager, they didn't let us out. They didn't want to know that we should know what really happened there. We had to send back postcards to the ghetto, that we are all together, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that the families are all all right, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: They made you write postcards?

speaker: interviewee
text: They made us write postcards back to the ghetto to-- we had two aunts there. Everybody had to write back, to the ghetto, postcards. In January, 194-- yeah, in July, '44. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On the beginning of July, '44, there were rumors already, even a little bit before, they took transports of men to send to work. They-- as far as I remember, every transport consisted of 1,000 men and 1,000 woman. And in one of the transport was Walter's brother too. He was three years older. They sent him to work. And it came around the 5th or 6th of July. They gave an order that Mengele is coming and make a selection. A selection meant that he was standing there. He had always a stick in his hand, to make to left and right. And they made-- they said they want only children, and the ages of 13 to 16, to come. We didn't know what means a selection, but they had-- we had to go in front of a barrack. And I took my brother. There was a lot of children. I had a feeling, because the fire that I saw at night, I didn't feel kosher. I felt that something is wrong. I never imagined, in my life, that they take live people and put them in the gas chambers, but I had a bad feeling. And I tried, on the selection, to push my brother. Mengele-- he made left or right, left or right. And we children-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they selected about 80 to 90 children to one side. And my brother, the little one, went to the other side. And I always-- I screamed to him Leo, go. Go again. And he went three times, and they didn't select him. But they selected Walter and me. My father was there too. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They took the 80-- the 80 or 90 children, and they-- we had to march to the front gate. When we marched to the front the gate, I remember the parents of the children-- including my mother, my father, my uncle, my brother, my cousin-- they all marched after us, because they thought we're going to work-- they will send us to work, to say goodbye. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My mother was a little woman. My father was a tall man. And I could see them walking after us and crying. And they were so helpless. And with their hand, they made us goodbye. And they didn't know what to do, what-- what will be, how they will see us. And we are marching. We always turned our head back, and when we came to the front gate, to the square, they put us all together, all 89 children. And we stood there, and my cousin, Walter, told me, Ernst, I'm going back to my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, because I had a bad feeling already, I grabbed his shirt, and I says, Walter, you go with me. I don't know what our [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know what our future will be. But I have a feeling you have to go with me. And he-- he wanted to go, and he pushed me away, and says, Ernst, how can you be so mean to me? I want to go back to my mother. And they start running. I grab him again, and I hit him. I-- I hit him. I says, Walter, you go with me. I don't know what will be, but you have to be with me. And he many times said, how can you be so mean? I want to go back to my mother. Why you don't let me?

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, Walter, you go with me. I don't know what will be. We have to be together. And that was the last time that I saw my mother, my father, and my brother. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And we had to go. They marched us from B Lager to [GERMAN]. In [GERMAN], they put us in, in block 13. That was the [GERMAN], block 13 and 11. It was 11 and 13. And one was the [GERMAN], and the other one was the [GERMAN]. That is the people that worked in the crematoriums. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What I forgot to tell you was, when we came to Auschwitz-- to Birkenau-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] after they cleaned us, I mean-- we had to go in Lysol water and everything-- and before we marched in the B Lager, they made us numbers of the hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you show us the number?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they put us numbers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the number?

speaker: interviewee
text: My number was 82136.

speaker: interviewer
text: Put your arm over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Walter's number was 82134. And between Walter and me was 82135 was oh, Walter's brother or my brother. I don't remember exactly. But all our-- all-- his father, my father-- everybody had our numbers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when they put the number on your arm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt terr-- first of all, it was painful. They stuck needles in it, and it was very painful for me to-- to put it in. But I felt, from that moment on, I didn't have anymore name, anyway. I was a number. I became like a-- like a number. Even an animal, you give a name. A dog you give a name. But we didn't have anymore names. Since this moment on, there was no name. Everything-- we were always called by the number-- 821--. [GERMAN]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When they put us in this block in, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the first four or five days, or six days, we had-- they were very friendly to us. We do-- we did exercise. I don't know what they had in their mind-- or they wanted to send us to another European country, to show they have-- they don't kill children. Or they wanted to bring in the Red Cross. I cannot figure out, til today, why we were selected, what was the reason. But the first week, it was-- everything was all right. It was-- then, right on the beginning, we knew about the crematoriums. The first one or two days that we were there, between-- we-- I think we were in E Lager or D Lager. Between us and the camp of our parents was a camp of Hungarian womans.
